title: ACAS Guide: Managing Redundancy
topicLabel: Making roles redundant fairly and lawfully
chapters: Non-statutory guidance · Published by ACAS · Covers individual and collective redundancy procedures, consultation obligations, selection criteria, suitable alternative employment, and redundancy pay
desc: The ACAS guide on managing redundancy provides comprehensive practical guidance on handling redundancy situations fairly and lawfully. Redundancy is one of the five potentially fair reasons for dismissal under ERA 1996 s.98(2)(c), but the dismissal must also be fair in all the circumstances under s.98(4). The guide covers the legal definition of redundancy (ERA 1996 s.139), the obligation to consult individually with affected employees, the collective consultation obligations under TULRCA 1992 s.188 (30+ employees: 45 days; 20–29 employees: 30 days), fair selection criteria, the duty to consider suitable alternative employment, time off to look for work (ERA 1996 s.52), and the calculation of statutory redundancy pay. The Employment Rights Act 2025 introduces amendments to collective redundancy consultation requirements. Enhanced redundancy protections for pregnant employees and those on family leave are already in force under the Protection from Redundancy (Pregnancy and Family Leave) Act 2023.
legalStatus: Non-statutory guidance. Tribunals regularly refer to ACAS guidance as evidence of good practice when assessing the fairness of redundancy dismissals under ERA 1996 s.98(4).

sections.title: Legal definition and when the duty to consult arises
sections.summary: Redundancy arises under ERA 1996 s.139 when the employer has ceased or intends to cease carrying on the business, when the business has ceased or intends to cease in the place where the employee is employed, or when the requirements for employees to carry out work of a particular kind have ceased or diminished or are expected to. A genuine redundancy situation must exist — if the employer is using 'redundancy' as a pretext for dismissing an employee for another reason, the dismissal will be unfair. The duty to consult arises before any decisions are made about who will be made redundant. Individual consultation is required in all redundancy situations. Collective consultation under TULRCA 1992 s.188 is required where the employer proposes to dismiss 20 or more employees at one establishment within 90 days.
sections.text:
An employee is dismissed by reason of redundancy if the dismissal is wholly or mainly attributable to:

(a) the fact that the employer has ceased or intends to cease to carry on the business for the purposes of which the employee was employed, or has ceased or intends to cease to carry on that business in the place where the employee was so employed; or

(b) the fact that the requirements of that business for employees to carry out work of a particular kind, or for employees to carry out work of a particular kind in the place where the employee was employed, have ceased or diminished or are expected to cease or diminish.

Collective consultation obligations under TULRCA 1992 s.188 are triggered where an employer proposes to dismiss as redundant 20 or more employees at one establishment within a period of 90 days or less:
• 20–29 employees: consultation must begin at least 30 days before the first dismissal takes effect
• 100 or more employees: consultation must begin at least 45 days before the first dismissal takes effect

The obligation is to consult with appropriate representatives (recognised trade union representatives or elected employee representatives) about ways of avoiding the dismissals, reducing the numbers to be dismissed, and mitigating the consequences.

versions.description: ERA 1996 s.139 consolidated the redundancy definition from the Redundancy Payments Act 1965.
Collective consultation period for 100+ redundancies reduced from 90 days to 45 days by the Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992 (Amendment) Order 2013.
Protection from Redundancy (Pregnancy and Family Leave) Act 2023 extended enhanced redundancy protections to cover pregnancy period and 18 months post-birth.

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 contains provisions amending collective redundancy consultation requirements. The 'at one establishment' limitation may be removed, meaning the 20-employee threshold would apply across the entire business rather than per establishment. This is a significant change for multi-site employers.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: Enacted. Not yet in force. Subject to commencement orders.
pendingAmendment.anticipatedDate: TBC — subject to Secretary of State commencement order
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: Dismissal category. Removal of establishment limitation significantly increases collective consultation exposure for multi-site employers including FM sector operators like the Northerly Hill demo entity.

sections.eraNote: ERA 2025 proposes to remove the 'at one establishment' limitation in TULRCA 1992 s.188, meaning collective consultation obligations would be triggered by 20+ proposed redundancies across the entire business. For multi-site FM operators, this would mean that redundancies across Cardiff, Swansea, Bristol and Birmingham depots would be aggregated for consultation threshold purposes. Forward Intelligence classification: HIGH (Royal Assent achieved).

leadingCases.name: Murray v Foyle Meats Ltd [1999] UKHL 36
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that the redundancy definition requires a two-stage approach: first, has the requirement for employees to carry out work of a particular kind ceased or diminished? Second, was the dismissal attributable wholly or mainly to that state of affairs? The focus should be on the employer's requirement for work, not on the individual employee's role.
leadingCases.significance: Definitive authority on the correct approach to the statutory redundancy definition — focuses on the employer's requirements, not the employee's job

leadingCases.name: Safeway Stores plc v Burrell [1997] ICR 523
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT established the three-stage test for redundancy: (1) was the employee dismissed? (2) had the employer's requirements for employees to carry out work of a particular kind ceased or diminished? (3) was the dismissal caused wholly or mainly by that state of affairs?
leadingCases.significance: Established the three-stage redundancy test subsequently approved by the House of Lords in Murray v Foyle Meats

leadingCases.name: USDAW v Ethel Austin Ltd (in administration) [2015] ICR 675
leadingCases.heldText: Following the ECJ ruling in Lyttle (Case C-392/13), the Court of Appeal held that 'establishment' in the collective consultation provisions means the local employment unit to which the redundant workers are assigned, not the entire undertaking. This interpretation limits the scope of collective consultation obligations for multi-site employers.
leadingCases.significance: Key authority on the meaning of 'establishment' in collective consultation — ERA 2025 proposes to override this interpretation

refs: ERA 1996 s.139 (definition of redundancy)
ERA 1996 s.98(2)(c) (redundancy as potentially fair reason for dismissal)
TULRCA 1992 s.188 (duty to consult on collective redundancies)
TULRCA 1992 s.189 (protective award for failure to consult — up to 90 days' pay per employee)
Employment Rights Act 2025 (proposed removal of 'at one establishment' limitation)
https://www.acas.org.uk/manage-staff-redundancies

sections.title: Selection criteria, pooling, and individual consultation
sections.summary: The employer must use fair and objective selection criteria. Commonly used criteria include skills, qualifications, and experience; attendance and disciplinary records (excluding pregnancy-related absence); performance assessments; and length of service (though last-in-first-out alone may constitute indirect age discrimination). The employer must define a fair selection pool — the group of employees from which selections will be made. The pool should include all employees doing similar work or work that is interchangeable. Individual consultation must take place with each employee at risk, including warning of the risk of redundancy, explaining the selection criteria, allowing the employee to comment and challenge, and considering any representations. Failure to consult individually will normally render the dismissal unfair.
sections.text:
Employers should use fair and objective selection criteria. These might include:
• skills, qualifications and experience relevant to the roles that will continue
• attendance records (excluding absences related to pregnancy, disability, or other protected characteristics)
• disciplinary records
• performance assessments and appraisals
• length of service (though reliance on this alone may constitute indirect age discrimination)

The selection pool should include all employees doing similar work or work that is interchangeable. Drawing the pool too narrowly may render the selection unfair.

Individual consultation must include:
• warning the employee that they are at risk of redundancy
• explaining the business reasons for the redundancies
• explaining the selection criteria and how they have been applied to the employee
• considering any alternative proposals the employee may make
• considering whether suitable alternative employment exists within the organisation
• allowing the employee to be accompanied at consultation meetings

leadingCases.name: Williams v Compair Maxam Ltd [1982] ICR 156
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT set out guidelines for fair redundancy selection where a recognised trade union exists: the employer should give as much warning as possible, consult with the union about selection criteria, use objective criteria that can be independently checked, ensure selection is fairly applied, and consider whether suitable alternative employment is available.
leadingCases.significance: The leading authority on fair redundancy procedure — the 'Williams guidelines' are still cited in virtually every redundancy case

leadingCases.name: Rolls Royce plc v Unite the Union [2009] EWCA Civ 387
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that using length of service as one of several selection criteria for redundancy does not constitute unlawful age discrimination. Length of service is a proportionate means of achieving legitimate aims including rewarding loyalty and experience. However, using LIFO as the sole criterion would be more difficult to justify.
leadingCases.significance: Confirmed that length of service is a lawful selection criterion when used as part of a matrix, not as sole criterion

leadingCases.name: Mogane v Bradford Teaching Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust [2022] UKEAT/0142/21
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT emphasised the importance of individual consultation in the redundancy process. The employer must genuinely consult with the individual employee about the proposed redundancy, consider alternatives, and not simply go through the motions. Consultation must occur while proposals are still at a formative stage.
leadingCases.significance: Recent authority reinforcing the requirement for genuine individual consultation — not a box-ticking exercise

refs: ERA 1996 s.98(4) (test of fairness — selection criteria and consultation as part of reasonableness assessment)
ERA 1996 s.105 (automatically unfair selection for redundancy — protected reasons)
EqA 2010 s.19 (indirect age discrimination — LIFO selection criterion)
EqA 2010 s.13 (direct discrimination — selection criteria must not discriminate)
MPL 1999 reg.10 (priority right to suitable alternative vacancy for employees on maternity leave)
https://www.acas.org.uk/manage-staff-redundancies

sections.title: Suitable alternative employment and redundancy pay
sections.summary: The employer must consider whether suitable alternative employment exists within the organisation or any associated employer. An employee who unreasonably refuses an offer of suitable alternative employment loses their right to a statutory redundancy payment. A trial period of four weeks (or longer if agreed in writing for retraining purposes) applies when an employee accepts alternative employment. Statutory redundancy pay is calculated based on the employee's age, length of continuous service (up to 20 years), and a week's pay (capped, reviewed annually). The current weekly cap (from 6 April 2025) should be verified. Employees with two or more years' continuous service are entitled to statutory redundancy pay: 0.5 week's pay for each complete year of service aged under 22; 1 week's pay for each year aged 22–40; 1.5 weeks' pay for each year aged 41 and over.
sections.text:
Employers must consider whether suitable alternative employment exists. An offer of suitable alternative employment should be made before the existing contract ends. The employee is entitled to a trial period of four weeks in the new role.

Statutory redundancy pay is calculated as:
• 0.5 week's pay for each complete year of service while the employee was under 22
• 1 week's pay for each complete year of service while the employee was 22 or older but under 41
• 1.5 weeks' pay for each complete year of service while the employee was 41 or older

Maximum 20 years' service counts. A week's pay is subject to a statutory cap (reviewed annually on 6 April). The employee must have two or more years' continuous service to qualify.

Employees under notice of redundancy are entitled to reasonable time off during working hours to look for new employment or arrange training (ERA 1996 s.52). The employee must have two years' continuous service. Paid at 40% of a week's pay for a day's absence.

versions.description: ERA 1996 Part XI consolidated the redundancy payments scheme from the Redundancy Payments Act 1965.

leadingCases.name: Thomas & Betts Manufacturing Ltd v Harding [1980] IRLR 255
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that in assessing whether alternative employment is 'suitable', the tribunal should consider the terms of the new contract objectively. In assessing whether the employee's refusal is 'reasonable', the tribunal should consider the employee's personal circumstances subjectively. Suitability is objective; reasonableness of refusal is subjective.
leadingCases.significance: Established the objective/subjective distinction between suitability of the offer and reasonableness of refusal

refs: ERA 1996 ss.135–145 (right to redundancy payment)
ERA 1996 s.141 (renewal of contract or re-engagement — suitable alternative employment)
ERA 1996 s.138 (trial period in new employment — 4 weeks)
ERA 1996 s.52 (right to time off to look for work or arrange training)
ERA 1996 s.162 (calculation of redundancy payment)
ERA 1996 s.227 (limits on a week's pay — statutory cap)
Employment Rights (Increase of Limits) Order (annual uprating)
https://www.acas.org.uk/manage-staff-redundancies

pendingAmendments: ERA 2025 — removal of 'at one establishment' from TULRCA s.188 (expected Oct 2026)
ERA 2025 — doubling of protective award to 180 days (expected April 2026)

parliamentaryOrigin.statutoryBasis: Non-statutory guidance. However, compliance with ACAS redundancy guidance is relevant to the fairness assessment in unfair dismissal proceedings (ERA 1996 s.98(4)) and to the adequacy of collective consultation under TULRCA 1992 s.188.
parliamentaryOrigin.note: ACAS redundancy guidance covers the statutory framework (collective consultation under TULRCA s.188, individual consultation, selection criteria, alternative employment, notice periods) and best practice. The guidance reflects the case law on fair redundancy procedures (Williams v Compair Maxam, Polkey).

precedingDocuments.title: TULRCA 1992 ss.188-198
precedingDocuments.significance: The statutory framework for collective redundancy consultation — 20+ redundancies at one establishment within 90 days.

precedingDocuments.title: ERA 1996 ss.135-165
precedingDocuments.significance: Individual redundancy rights: right to redundancy payment, right not to be unfairly selected for redundancy, right to time off to look for work.

leadingCases.name: Williams v Compair Maxam Ltd [1982] ICR 156
leadingCases.heldText: Established the principles of fair redundancy: warning, consultation, objective selection criteria, and consideration of alternative employment.
leadingCases.significance: Foundation authority for fair redundancy procedures.

leadingCases.name: Susie Radin Ltd v GMB [2004] EWCA Civ 180
leadingCases.heldText: Maximum protective award is the starting point for collective consultation failure — reduction only for mitigating circumstances.

leadingCases.name: USDAW v Ethel Austin [2015] ICR 675
leadingCases.heldText: 'Establishment' means local employment unit. ERA 2025 will override this.
